# CartoBoost

CartoBoost is a Python regression toolkit with Rust native bindings for
temporal, spatial, geotemporal, graph, and neural-embedding modeling.

## Primary Docs

- README: ./README.md
- Documentation home: ./docs/index.md
- Docusaurus config: ./docusaurus.config.ts
- Docusaurus sidebar: ./sidebars.ts
- Docs homepage component: ./src/pages/index.tsx
- Docs theme stylesheet: ./src/css/custom.css
- Installation: ./docs/installation.md
- Getting started: ./docs/getting-started.md
- Choose a model: ./docs/user-guide/model-types.md
- Model guides: ./docs/user-guide/forecasting-models/index.md
- ARIMA and AutoARIMA guide: ./docs/user-guide/forecasting-models/arima.md
- ARIMA examples: ./docs/user-guide/forecasting-models/arima-examples.md
- Python API reference: ./docs/reference/python-api.md
- Parameters: ./docs/user-guide/parameters.md
- Spatial modeling: ./docs/spatial_modeling.md
- Graph models and features: ./docs/graph-features.md
- Neural embedding models and features: ./docs/neural-features.md
- Benchmarks: ./docs/benchmarks/index.md
- Forecasting benchmarks and M4/M5/M6 evidence: ./docs/benchmarks/forecasting.md
- Forecasting overhaul: ./docs/forecasting_overhaul.md

## Public Python Entry Points

- `cartoboost.CartoBoostRegressor`: sklearn-style tabular regressor.
- `cartoboost.NeuralEmbeddingStandaloneRegressor`: direct supervised ID
  embedding regressor.
- `cartoboost.NeuralEmbeddingRegressor`: optional neural feature-generation
  workflow for tabular modeling.
- `cartoboost.Node2VecStandaloneRegressor`: direct node2vec graph regressor.
- `cartoboost.GraphSageStandaloneRegressor`: direct homogeneous GraphSAGE graph
  regressor.
- `cartoboost.HeteroGraphSageStandaloneRegressor`: direct typed-edge GraphSAGE
  graph regressor.
- `cartoboost.HinSageStandaloneRegressor`: direct typed-node and typed-relation
  HinSAGE graph regressor.
- `cartoboost.Node2VecLinkPredictor`, `cartoboost.GraphSageLinkPredictor`,
  `cartoboost.HeteroGraphSageLinkPredictor`, and
  `cartoboost.HinSageLinkPredictor`: standalone source-target link predictors.

## Optional Integrations

- `cartoboost[h3]`: H3 latitude/longitude encoding helpers; deterministic
  validation and sparse-row assembly are Rust-backed.
- `cartoboost[s2]`: S2 latitude/longitude encoding helpers; deterministic
  validation and sparse-row assembly are Rust-backed.
- `cartoboost[duckdb]`: DuckDB relation inputs.
- `cartoboost[polars]`: Polars inputs.
- `cartoboost[explain]`: SHAP explanation helpers.
- `cartoboost[optuna]`: Optuna tuning helpers.
- `cartoboost[onnx]`: ONNX export for the supported dense axis-tree subset.

## Usage Notes For LLMs

- Prefer taxi-style examples: pickup zones, dropoff zones, pickup-dropoff pairs,
  trip distance, fare, duration, and demand.
- Treat graph and neural standalone models as first-class direct APIs. Do not
  require `CartoBoostRegressor` when explaining standalone graph or neural usage.
- Use `GraphFeatureTransformer` and `NeuralEmbeddingFeatures` only when the user
  explicitly wants generated columns for another estimator.
- Keep algorithmic behavior in Rust crates under `crates/`; Python modules under
  `python/cartoboost` should stay thin ergonomic wrappers.
- Use `cartoboost.kalman_filter` or `local_level_kalman_filter` when users need
  Kalman state diagnostics, smoothed states, covariance/variance, gains,
  residual metrics, likelihood, or forecast intervals in addition to point
  forecasts.
- Use `LocalLevelKalmanForecaster`, `KalmanForecaster`,
  `AutoLocalLevelKalmanForecaster`, or `AutoKalmanForecaster` when users need
  frame-based fitting/prediction through `cartoboost.forecasting`; auto variants
  tune variance grids in Rust and expose `selected_params` plus
  `validation_scores` metadata.
- For Kalman docs or examples, prefer taxi pickup-demand examples with
  filtered/smoothed state plots, forecast interval bands, and standardized
  innovation charts; the runnable plotting example is
  `examples/forecasting/kalman_diagnostics_visualization.py`.
- Forecasting model examples with runnable visual diagnostics live under
  `examples/forecasting/`: `naive_seasonal_visualization.py`,
  `theta_optimized_visualization.py`, `ets_component_visualization.py`,
  `arima_example_visualization.py`, `cartoboost_lag_visualization.py`,
  `weighted_ensemble_visualization.py`, and `kriging_example_visualization.py`.
- Use `cartoboost.forecasting.AutoForecaster` for deterministic direct,
  decomposition, AutoStats, reconciliation, probabilistic, neural, and ensemble
  forecasting experiments; benchmark claims must cite the maintained artifact.
- Use `cartoboost.metrics.wrmsse`, `cartoboost.metrics.m6`, and the benchmark
  artifact `official_metrics` section for M5/M6-style checks instead of
  treating all forecasting benchmarks as RMSE-only. M6 benchmark artifacts
  include training-window rank-probability calibration metadata.
- New forecasting benchmark artifacts expose `git_commit`, `dataset_hash`,
  `source_file_hashes`, `benchmark_integrity`, and `resource_usage` for
  provenance checks.
- Use `uv sync --group dev` for local development and
  `uv run --group dev maturin develop` after native binding changes.
- Use `npm run typecheck` and `npm run build` for the Docusaurus docs site.
- Run `just validate` for full validation when feasible.
- Benchmark claims need equal settings, serious baselines, stable splits, and
  enough repeatability evidence to support the claim.
